questions.text: What are vinyl alcohol and acetaldehyde?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is repeated from option A. It suggests that vinyl alcohol and acetaldehyde are position isomers. However, this is not an accurate description of their relationship.
Vinyl alcohol and acetaldehyde are not functional group isomers of each other.

Metamers are a type of structural isomerism where molecules have the same molecular formula but differ in the connectivity of their atoms. Vinyl alcohol and acetaldehyde do have the same molecular formula (C₂H₄O) but differ in the arrangement of atoms, making them metamers.

Tautomers are a type of structural isomerism where molecules exist in equilibrium between two different structural forms that can rapidly interconvert. Vinyl alcohol and acetaldehyde are tautomers of each other. In other words, they can transform into each other by the movement of a hydrogen atom and a double bond.

questions.explanation: The correct description for the relationship between vinyl alcohol and acetaldehyde is D. Tautomers. They are tautomeric forms of each other, which means they can rapidly interconvert.

questions.text: What is the common name of 1,2,3-propanetriol?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Butyl alcohol refers to a group of alcohols derived from butane, which has four carbon atoms. It is not the common name for 1,2,3-propanetriol.
Glycerol is a term used to describe a class of compounds that contain two hydroxyl groups (-OH) on adjacent carbon atoms. It is not the common name for 1,2,3-propanetriol.

The common name of 1, 2, 3-propanetriol is glycerol. Glycerol is a trihydroxy alcohol commonly used in various industries, including food, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics. It is also known as glycerine.
Propyl alcohol, also known as propanol, refers to a group of alcohols derived from propane, which have three carbon atoms. It is not the common name for 1,2,3-propanetriol.

questions.explanation: The common name of 1, 2, 3-propanetriol is glycerol. Glycerol is a trihydroxy alcohol commonly used in various industries, including food, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics. It is also known as glycerine.

questions.text: Both alcohol and phenol contain
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Alcohol and phenol are organic compounds that contain the hydroxyl functional group (OH). This group consists of an oxygen atom bonded to a hydrogen atom. It is responsible for the characteristic properties and reactivity of alcohols and phenols.

This option is incorrect because the carboxyl functional group (COOH) consists of a carbonyl group (C=O) and a hydroxyl group (OH) bonded to the same carbon atom. Carboxylic acids contain this group, but it is not present in alcohols or phenols.

This option is incorrect because the CH2 group is simply a methylene group, which consists of a carbon atom bonded to two hydrogen atoms. While it may be present in some organic compounds, it is not a defining characteristic of alcohols or phenols.

This option is incorrect because the CHO group refers to an aldehyde functional group. Aldehydes have a carbonyl group (C=O) bonded to a hydrogen atom. While aldehydes can be derived from alcohols through oxidation, the CHO group itself is not present in alcohols or phenols.

questions.explanation: Alcohol and phenol are organic compounds that contain the hydroxyl functional group (OH). This group consists of an oxygen atom bonded to a hydrogen atom. It is responsible for the characteristic properties and reactivity of alcohols and phenols.

options.explanation: This is an incorrect term.

The correct answer is:Carbolic acidExplanation:Phenol (C₆H₅OH) is commonly known as carbolic acid.It was historically used as an antiseptic and disinfectant.The name "carbolic acid" comes from its acidic nature and its early extraction from coal tar.
This is an incorrect term.

questions.explanation: The other name for phenol is Carbolic acid.

questions.text: Why is phenol more acidic than Alcohol?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Due to the presence of resonance in phenol
options.explanation: It is true that resonance makes phenol more acidic as it allows delocalization of the negative charge. Hence, this statement is correct, just as option B is. So we cannot mark it as correct.

options.text: Due to the absence of resonance in alcohol
options.explanation: Alcohols do not have resonance in them because there is no conjugation and no ring. This is why alcohols are less acidic than phenols. Hence, this statement is also correct. But we cannot mark it correct because option A is also true.

options.text: Both Options A and B are correct
options.explanation: As both options A and B are correct, this option will be marked correct.

options.text: Because in phenol, hydrogen is attached to oxygen,
options.explanation: This is not a valid reason, and it does not affect the acidity of phenol. So this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Phenol is more acidic than alcohol due to the stabilization of the phenoxide ion through resonance. The presence of an electron-withdrawing group increases the acidity of phenol by stabilizing the phenoxide ion.

questions.text: Glycerol can also be termed as?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 1 - butanol: Incorrect because 1-butanol is a four-carbon straight-chain alcohol, while glycerol (also known as glycerin or glycerine) is a three-carbon compound, making them different chemical entities.

Glycerol contains three carbons, giving it the “propane” part to its name. It has an OH group attached to each carbon which account for “1,2,3- triol” Hence, option B is correct as it has 3 hydroxyl groups.
2 - methyl - propanol: Incorrect because 2-methyl-propanol is an isomer of butanol and not related to glycerol in terms of its chemical structure and properties.

Isobutyl alcohol: Incorrect because isobutyl alcohol is also an isomer of butanol and has a different chemical structure from glycerol, which is a trihydroxy alcohol with three hydroxyl groups on a three-carbon chain.

questions.explanation: Glycerol contains three carbons, giving it the “propane” part to its name. It has an OH group attached to each carbon which account for “1,2,3- triol” Hence, option B is correct as it has 3 hydroxyl groups.

questions.text: What is the other name of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The other name of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol is picric acid. It is a yellow, crystalline solid that is highly explosive. It is also a strong acid and can cause severe burns. The name "picric" comes from the Greek word "pikros", which means "bitter". This is because picric acid has a very bitter taste. Picric acid was first synthesized in 1771 by Carl Wilhelm Scheele. It was originally used as a yellow dye, but it was later found to be too explosive for this purpose. Today, picric acid is used mainly as an explosive. It is also used in some fireworks and as a component of some smokeless powders.
Nitrophenol is a general term for any phenol that has been nitrated.
TNT is a specific compound that is made up of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene.
Benzophenone is a different compound altogether. It is a colorless, crystalline solid that is used in perfumes and plastics.

questions.explanation: Correct option is A. The other name of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol is picric acid. It is a yellow, crystalline solid that is highly explosive. It is also a strong acid and can cause severe burns. The name "picric" comes from the Greek word "pikros", which means "bitter". This is because picric acid has a very bitter taste. Picric acid was first synthesized in 1771 by Carl Wilhelm Scheele. It was originally used as a yellow dye, but it was later found to be too explosive for this purpose. Today, picric acid is used mainly as an explosive. It is also used in some fireworks and as a component of some smokeless powders.

questions.text: In IUPAC naming, alcohols are named as _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Alkanal is not a name given to alcohols in IUPAC nomenclature. Alkanals are a class of organic compounds that contain a carbonyl group (C=O) at the end of a carbon chain.
Alkyl alcohols is not a specific name given to alcohols in IUPAC nomenclature, but it can be used to describe any alcohol that contains an Alkyl group attached to the hydroxyl (-OH) functional group.
In IUPAC naming, alcohols are named Alkanols and they are represented in any reaction through the general formula R-OH.

Alkenol is not a name given to alcohol in IUPAC nomenclature. Alkenols are a class of organic compounds that contains a hydroxyl (-OH) functional group attached to an alkene (unsaturated hydrocarbon) chain.

questions.explanation: IUPAC names alcohols as alkanols and they are represented in any reaction through the general formula R-OH.

questions.text: Which of the following is termed as hydroxyl derivative of alkanes?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Carboxylic acids are organic compounds that contain a carboxyl group (-COOH) attached to a carbon atom of an alkyl or aryl group. The general formula for a carboxylic acid is R-COOH, where R represents an alkyl or aryl group.

Aldehydes are organic compounds that contain a carbonyl group (-CHO) attached to a carbon atom of an alkyl or aryl group. The general formula for an aldehyde is R-CHO, where R represents an alkyl or aryl group.

Phenols and ethers are both types of organic compounds. Phenols are organic compounds that contain a hydroxyl group (-OH) attached to a benzene ring. Ethers are organic compounds that contain an oxygen atom (-O-) bonded to two alkyl or aryl groups.

Hydroxyl derivatives of alkanes are known as alcohols. They are organic compounds that contain a hydroxyl group (-OH) attached to a carbon atom of an alkane chain. The general formula for an alcohol is R-OH, where R represents an alkyl or aryl group.

questions.explanation: Carboxylic acids are organic compounds that contain a carboxyl group (-COOH) attached to a carbon atom of an alkyl or aryl group. The general formula for a carboxylic acid is R-COOH, where R represents an alkyl or aryl group. Aldehydes are organic compounds that contain a carbonyl group (-CHO) attached to a carbon atom of an alkyl or aryl group. The general formula for an aldehyde is R-CHO, where R represents an alkyl or aryl group. Phenols and ethers are both types of organic compounds. Phenols are organic compounds that contain a hydroxyl group (-OH) attached to a benzene ring. Ethers are organic compounds that contain an oxygen atom (-O-) bonded to two alkyl or aryl groups. Hydroxyl derivatives of alkanes are known as alcohols. They are organic compounds that contain a hydroxyl group (-OH) attached to a carbon atom of an alkane chain. The general formula for an alcohol is R-OH, where R represents an alkyl or aryl group.

questions.text: In IUPAC naming alcohols are named as _ ?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.Alkanal refers to aldehydes, which are organic compounds that contain the –CHO (carbonyl) functional group.
This option is incorrect.An alkyl alcohol refers to an alcohol (–OH group) attached to an alkyl group (R–).

In IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) naming, alcohols are named as alkanols. Alkanols are a class of organic compounds that contain a hydroxyl (-OH) functional group attached to an alkane chain. The general formula for alkanols is CnH2n+1OH.

This option is incorrect.An alkenol is an organic compound that contains both a hydroxyl group (-OH) and a carbon-carbon double bond (C=C) in the same molecule. It is a combination of an alkene and an alcohol functional group.

questions.explanation: In IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) naming, alcohols are named as alkanols. Alkanols are a class of organic compounds that contain a hydroxyl (-OH) functional group attached to an alkane chain. The general formula for alkanols is CnH2n+1OH.

questions.text: Organic compounds that are considered as derivatives of water are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: a) Aldehydes & KetonesExplanation: Aldehydes and ketones are organic compounds that contain a carbonyl group (C=O) as their functional group. They are not considered derivatives of water.

b) Alcohols and PhenolsExplanation: Alcohols and phenols are organic compounds that contain hydroxyl groups (-OH) as their functional group. Alcohols have the general formula R-OH, while phenols have the general formula Ar-OH (where R represents an alkyl group, and Ar represents an aromatic ring). These compounds can be considered as derivatives of water because they are derived from water by replacing one hydrogen atom with an alkyl or aromatic group.

c) Phenols and EthersExplanation: We have already discussed phenols in option (b). Ethers, on the other hand, are organic compounds that contain an oxygen atom bridging two alkyl or aryl groups. The general formula for ethers is R-O-R' (where R and R' represent alkyl or aryl groups). Ethers can also be considered as derivatives of water because they are derived from water by replacing both hydrogen atoms with alkyl or aryl groups.
d) Carboxylic acidsExplanation: Carboxylic acids are organic compounds that contain a carboxyl group (-COOH) as their functional group. They are not considered derivatives of water.

questions.explanation: Alcohols, phenols, and ethers are much closer to water in structure and hence are considered as derivatives of water.

questions.text: Which of the following is TRUE regarding Methyl Alcohol?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: It is a colorless, volatile, thin liquid with specific gravity 0.796 at 15°C.
options.explanation: This is the correct option as it accurately describes the physical properties of methyl alcohol, also known as methanol.

options.text: It is used for low-temperature thermometers and as a fuel substitute.
options.explanation: These are characteristics of Ethanol, not Methyl Alcohol. Methyl Alcohol is toxic and primarily used as an industrial solvent and antifreeze.

options.text: It is extensively used in the formation of different beverages.
options.explanation: These are characteristics of Ethanol. Methyl Alcohol is toxic and not suitable for consumption.

options.text: It is used as a base for perfumes.
options.explanation: These are characteristics of esters. Methyl Alcohol is not used in perfumes due to its toxicity.

options.text: It is used as an antiseptic and disinfectant.
options.explanation: These are characteristics of Phenol. Methyl Alcohol is not used for sanitization purposes due to its toxicity.

questions.explanation: Methyl Alcohol, also known as methanol, is a colorless, volatile, and thin liquid with a specific gravity of 0.796 at 15°C. This makes Option A the correct choice. Methyl Alcohol is primarily used as an industrial solvent and antifreeze, and is not suitable for consumption or use in perfumes due to its toxicity. Options B and C describe Ethanol, which is used in thermometers and beverages. Option D describes esters, often used in perfumes. Option E describes Phenol, which is used as an antiseptic and disinfectant.

questions.text: Which of the following compounds will give a secondary alcohol after reaction with NaBH4?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is a ketone, and its reduction (NaBH4 is a reducing agent) results in a secondary alcohol. This is because the carbonyl functional group is present in the middle of the chain so, the hydroxyl group is formed in the same place after reduction, giving a secondary alcohol.
Option B is an ester, which does not undergo reduction with NaBH.
Option C is an aldehyde, that forms a primary alcohol, upon reduction, as its carbonyl group is towards the terminal end of the chain, thereby forming a terminal hydroxyl group.
Option D is a carboxylic acid, which does not undergo reduction with NaBH4.

questions.explanation: Option A is a ketone, and its reduction (NaBH4 is a reducing agent) results in a secondary alcohol. questions.text: In the following reaction sequence, product D will be:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The above reaction scheme describes dehydrohalogenation of the alkyl halide into an alkene, followed by hydration of the alkene. The first step involves the reaction of propyl bromide with alcoholic KOH, which will result in elimination or dehydrohalogenation, whereby the halogen atom and adjacent carbon atom's hydrogen atom is removed, resulting in the formation of a double bond between the two carbons, forming propane, which is product C. Propene is acted upon by aqueous H2SO4, which implies hydration or addition of H2O, resulting in the formation of two products; the major product which is 2-propanol and the minor product which is 1-propanol so, the major product is the preferred option, i.e. option C.

questions.text: Select one which is alcohol.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: CH3-CH2-Br is an example of an alkyl halide (also known as haloalkanes) which are compounds in which one or more hydrogen atoms in an alkane have been replaced by halogen atoms (fluorine, chlorine, bromine or iodine).

Ethers are a class of organic compounds that contain an ether group-an oxygen atom connected to two alkyl or aryl groups. They have the general formula R-O-R', where R and R' represent the alkyl or aryl groups. Therefore CH3-O-CH3 is an ether.

CH3COOH is a carboxylic acid that is an organic acid which contains a carboxyl group (C(=O)OH) attached to an R- group. The general formula of a carboxylic acid is R-COOH, with R referring to the alkyl, alkenyl, aryl, or other group.
An alcohol is an organic compound that carries at least one hydroxyl functional group (-OH) bound to a saturated carbon atom, hence CH3-CH2-OH is an alcohol.

questions.explanation: An alcohol is an organic compound that carries at least one hydroxyl functional group (−OH) bound to a saturated carbon atom, hence CH3-CH2-OH is an alcohol. CH3-CH2-Br is an example of an alkyl halide (also known as haloalkanes) which are compounds in which one or more hydrogen atoms in an alkane have been replaced by halogen atoms (fluorine, chlorine, bromine or iodine). Ethers are a class of organic compounds that contain an ether group—an oxygen atom connected to two alkyl or aryl groups. They have the general formula R–O–R′, where R and R′ represent the alkyl or aryl groups. Therefore CH3-O-CH3 is an ether. CH3COOH is a carboxylic acid that is an organic acid which contains a carboxyl group (C(=O)OH) attached to an R-group. The general formula of a carboxylic acid is R–COOH, with R referring to the alkyl, alkenyl, aryl, or other group.

questions.explanation: Picric acid, also called 2,4,6-trinitrophenol, is a pale yellow, odorless, crystalline solid that has been used as a military explosive, as a yellow dye, and as an antiseptic. There are two functional groups in picric acids: OH and NO2. According to the priority list of Functional groups -COOH> -COOR> -CHO> -CO> -OH> -NH2 > Alkene/ Alkynes OH should be given priority, i.e., position 1, and then nitro groups are named accordingly. As OH is prioritized, we can use the name phenol as well. In this way, Nitro groups are given positions 2, 4, and 6, as illustrated below

questions.text: Ethanol-water is a/an _mixture.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: a) Azeotropic: This is the correct option. Ethanol-water mixture is an azeotropic mixture. An azeotropic mixture is a mixture of two or more liquids that has a constant boiling point and composition, meaning that the vapor phase has the same composition as the liquid phase.

b) Ideal: An ideal mixture refers to a mixture in which the interactions between the components are negligible. Ethanol-water mixture is not considered an ideal mixture due to the strong interactions between ethanol and water molecules.
c) Benedict's: Benedict's solution is a reagent used to test for the presence of reducing sugars. It is not related to the type of mixture formed by ethanol and water.

d) Aliphatic: Aliphatic compounds are organic compounds that consist of straight chains, branched chains, or non-aromatic rings of carbon atoms. Ethanol is an aliphatic compound, but the question is asking about the type of mixture, not the compound itself.

questions.explanation: It has a constant boiling point.

questions.text: Which one is an aromatic alcohol:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is a simple alcohol, but it is not aromatic.
This is a simple alcohol, but it is not aromatic.

An aromatic alcohol is an alcohol that contains an aromatic ring, such as a benzene ring, with a hydroxyl group (-OH) attached. Benzyl alcohol fits this definition, as it has a benzene ring with an attached hydroxyl group.
While p-cresol has an aromatic ring, it is not classified as an alcohol. It is a phenol, which is a compound where a hydroxyl group is directly attached to an aromatic ring.

questions.explanation: An aromatic alcohol is an alcohol that contains an aromatic ring, such as a benzene ring, with a hydroxyl group (-OH) attached. Benzyl alcohol fits this definition, as it has a benzene ring with an attached hydroxyl group. Therefore, option c is correct.

questions.text: Rectified spirit is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 100% ethanol, also known as absolute ethanol, is not classified as rectified spirit because it lacks the small percentage of water.
This is the correct composition of rectified spirit, consisting of 95% ethanol and 5% water.
90% ethanol contains a higher percentage of water than rectified spirit, thus it is not classified as such.
This option represents a highly diluted form of ethanol, far from the composition of rectified spirit.

questions.explanation: Rectified spirit, also known as neutral spirits, is a high-purity ethanol solution that has undergone multiple distillation processes known as rectification to reach a concentration of 95% ethanol and 5% water. This specific ratio allows it to be used effectively in applications requiring high-purity alcohol. Option A (100% ethanol) is incorrect because absolute ethanol does not contain any water, making it distinct from rectified spirit. Option C (90% ethanol) contains more water than rectified spirit, altering its properties and uses. Option D (35% ethanol) is far too diluted and is not used in contexts where rectified spirit is required.

questions.text: The isomerism exhibits by C2H11OH is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Position isomerism: C2H11OH, which represents a molecule with two carbon atoms, exhibits position isomerism. This is because the hydroxyl group (-OH) can be located on either of the two carbon atoms, resulting in different structural isomers.
Functional group isomerism: C2H11OH does not exhibit functional group isomerism. Functional group isomerism occurs when molecules have the same molecular formula but different functional groups.
Chain isomerism: C2H11OH does not exhibit chain isomerism. Chain isomerism occurs when molecules have the same molecular formula but different arrangements of the carbon chain.

questions.explanation: Position isomerism: C2H11OH, which represents a molecule with two carbon atoms, exhibits position isomerism. This is because the hydroxyl group (-OH) can be located on either of the two carbon atoms, resulting in different structural isomers. Therefore, the correct option is "Position isomerism" as C2H11OH can exhibit different structural isomers based on the position of the hydroxyl group.

Phenol is a colorless, crystalline, deliquescent solid with a particular phenolic odor having melting point 41 ° C and boiling point 182 ° C.

questions.explanation: One important thing which confuses student is that they intermix Phenyl Hydrazine and Phenyl Hydrazone. Hydrazone consists of carbon–nitrogen double bond i.e R1R2C=N−NH2. In Phenyl hydrazone, one H atom of hydrazone is replaced by phenyl ring. Now if we attach two nitro groups at position 2 and 4, as given in the question, the product we have is 2,4-Dinitrophenylhydrazone.

questions.text: The IUPAC name of isopropyl alcohol is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. 1-butanol is a four-carbon alcohol, whereas isopropyl alcohol has only three carbon atoms.
The IUPAC name of isopropyl alcohol is propan-2-ol, indicating it is a three-carbon chain alcohol with the hydroxyl group (-OH) attached to the second carbon atom.
This option is incorrect. 1-propanol has the hydroxyl group attached to the first carbon, not the second as in isopropyl alcohol.
This option is incorrect. 2-butanol has four carbon atoms, while isopropyl alcohol has only three.
This option is incorrect. 'Propanol' is not specific enough as it doesn't indicate the position of the hydroxyl group, which is crucial in IUPAC naming.

questions.explanation: The correct IUPAC name for isopropyl alcohol is propan-2-ol. This name reflects the structure of the molecule: a three-carbon chain (propane) with a hydroxyl group (-OH) attached to the second carbon atom. The prefix 'iso-' in isopropyl indicates the branching at the second carbon, which aligns with the IUPAC naming as 'propan-2-ol'.Option A (1-butanol) and Option D (2-butanol) are incorrect because they describe four-carbon alcohols, not three. Option C (1-propanol) is incorrect because the hydroxyl group is on the first carbon, not the second. Option E (propanol) is too vague as it doesn't specify the position of the hydroxyl group.

questions.text: The melting and boiling point of alcohols are high as compared to corresponding alkanes due to
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Dipole-dipole interactions are attractive forces that occur between polar molecules. These interactions arise from the presence of permanent dipoles within the molecules, which are created due to differences in electronegativity between atoms.

This option is correct. Hydrogen bonding is a specific type of attractive interaction that occurs between a hydrogen atom covalently bonded to a highly electronegative atom and another electronegative atom. This interaction is crucial in many chemical and biological processes. Thus due to presence of -OH they can form hydrogen bonding and this ability is highly significant in lower alcohols compared to higher alcohols and thus alcohols have high melting and boiling points compared to corresponding alkanes
This option is incorrect. Ionic interactions in alcohols primarily occur through the formation of hydrogen bonds

This option is incorrect. Van der Waals interactions are weak, non-covalent forces that arise from the interactions between molecules or parts of molecules. They are named after the Dutch scientist Johannes Diderik van der Waals, who studied these forces in the context of gases.

questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonding is a specific type of attractive interaction that occurs between a hydrogen atom covalently bonded to a highly electronegative atom and another electronegative atom. This interaction is crucial in many chemical and biological processes. Thus due to presence of -OH they can form hydrogen bonding and this ability is highly significant in lower alcohols compared to higher alcohols and thus alcohols have high melting and boiling points compared to corresponding alkanes

questions.text: Methanol is prepared from carbon monoxide and hydrogen. The catalyst used for this reaction is:

questions.text: Which of the following is not characteristic of alcohols?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Incorrect; while many alcohols are lighter than water, this is not the most distinguishing characteristic of alcohols.

options.text: Their boiling points rise fairly uniformly with increasing molecular weight
options.explanation: Incorrect; this is a typical property of alcohols and is more accurate than option c.

options.text: Lower members insoluble in water and organic solvents but solubility regularly increases with molecular weight
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because lower alcohols (such as methanol and ethanol) are soluble in water, not insoluble. As the molecular weight increases, alcohols become less soluble in water but are generally soluble in organic solvents. Therefore, the solubility trend is the opposite of what this option suggests.

options.text: Lower members have pleasant smell and burning taste, while higher members are odorless and tasteless
options.explanation: Incorrect; alcohols with a higher molecular weight tend to have less pleasant odors and more neutral characteristics, but this option generalizes the behavior incorrectly.

questions.explanation: This option is incorrect because lower alcohols (such as methanol and ethanol) are soluble in water, not insoluble. As the molecular weight increases, alcohols become less soluble in water but are generally soluble in organic solvents. Therefore, the solubility trend is the opposite of what this option suggests.

questions.text: acidic catalyzed hydration of alkenes except ethene leads to the formation of
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Primary alcohols are typically formed from primary carbocations, which are less stable and not favored in acidic catalyzed hydration of most alkenes.
While secondary and tertiary alcohols can be formed, the question asks for a mixture, not just one type.

options.text: mixture of primary and secondary alcohol
options.explanation: This option is unlikely because primary carbocations are rarely formed compared to secondary and tertiary carbocations in hydration reactions.

options.text: mixture of secondary and tertiary alcohol
options.explanation: Acidic catalyzed hydration typically involves carbocation intermediates where secondary and tertiary carbocations are more stable, leading to the formation of a mixture of secondary and tertiary alcohols.

questions.explanation: In the acidic catalyzed hydration of alkenes, the reaction proceeds through the formation of carbocations. Secondary and tertiary carbocations are more stable than primary carbocations due to hyperconjugation and inductive effects. Thus, the reaction typically leads to the formation of a mixture of secondary and tertiary alcohols. Primary alcohols are less commonly formed because primary carbocations are less stable and therefore not favored. Therefore, Option D is correct as it accurately describes the typical outcome of the reaction.

questions.text: a type of monohydric alcohol in which the hydroxyl bearing carbon is attached to one
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Primary alcohols have the hydroxyl group attached to a carbon atom that is connected to only one other carbon atom.
Tertiary alcohols have the hydroxyl group attached to a carbon atom that is connected to three other carbon atoms.
Secondary alcohols have the hydroxyl group attached to a carbon atom that is connected to two other carbon atoms.
Neo alcohols refer to a specific structure where the hydroxyl-bearing carbon is attached to one other carbon atom, typically in a branched-chain structure.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'neo alcohol,' which is a type of monohydric alcohol characterized by a hydroxyl-bearing carbon atom attached to one other carbon atom, often in a branched configuration. The term 'neo' refers to this specific structural classification. The other options describe different types of alcohols based on the number of carbon atoms connected to the hydroxyl-bearing carbon: primary alcohols (one carbon), secondary alcohols (two carbons), and tertiary alcohols (three carbons).

questions.text: another name of grain alcohol is
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Methanol is commonly known as wood alcohol, not grain alcohol.
Ethanol is the type of alcohol found in alcoholic beverages and is often referred to as grain alcohol.
1-Propanol is not referred to as grain alcohol. It is used as a solvent and in the pharmaceutical industry.
1-Butanol is not commonly known as grain alcohol. It is used as a solvent and in the production of butyl acetate.

questions.explanation: Ethanol, also known as ethyl alcohol, is the only type of alcohol that is called grain alcohol. It is produced by the fermentation of grains and is used in alcoholic beverages, as well as in some industrial applications. Methanol is known as wood alcohol due to its original method of production from wood. 1-Propanol and 1-Butanol have different industrial uses and are not referred to as grain alcohol.

questions.text: dentify the incorrect statement about physical properties or phenol:

options.text: It is colourless crystalline molecular solid
options.explanation: Phenol typically appears as a white crystalline solid, but it can also be colorless in its pure form. Therefore, this statement is incorrect as phenol is not a molecular solid in the same way that ionic or covalent solids are defined.

options.text: It is used as a disinfectant in hospitals and washrooms
options.explanation: Phenol has antibacterial properties and is used in various disinfectants, making this statement true.

options.text: It is sparingly soluble in water
options.explanation: Phenol is sparingly soluble in water but gets completely dissolved above 63.1 degree Celsius.
